title: Street bloco or sambódromo — two very different Carnivals
bodyMd:
Most of what a visitor actually experiences during Carnival is the **bloco de rua**: a free, open, first-come-first-served street party that anyone can walk into and follow. There's no ticket, no judging, and often no fixed route beyond a rough starting point (the *concentração*) and a general direction. You show up, you join in, you leave whenever you want.

The **sambódromo** parade is the opposite experience. It's ticketed, seated (in a *camarote* or *arquibancada*), and highly organized: each *escola de samba* has spent the whole year building floats, choreography, and a *samba-enredo* around one *enredo*, and a panel of judges scores every element of the performance. It's a competition with real stakes — relegation and promotion between divisions happen based on the results.

Both are "Carnival," but they call for different expectations: the bloco is participatory chaos, the sambódromo is a watched spectacle.

title: Why "pular carnaval" isn't just "celebrate"
bodyMd:
English "celebrate Carnival" makes it sound like an occasion you observe. **Pular carnaval** — literally "to jump Carnival" — describes something more physical and total: going out into the blocos, dancing for hours in the heat, following the music through the streets, losing track of the schedule entirely.

It implies full participation, not spectating. Someone who says "vou pular carnaval em Salvador" isn't planning to watch a parade from a chair — they're planning to be inside the crowd, sweaty and moving, for days. The related idiom **cair na folia** ("to fall into the revelry") captures the same idea: letting go completely rather than holding back.

title: Rio, Salvador, and Recife/Olinda are three different Carnivals
bodyMd:
Brazil doesn't have one Carnival — it has several, each built around a different core experience:

- **Rio de Janeiro**: famous for the sambódromo parades — competitive, choreographed, escola-de-samba spectacle, alongside hundreds of free street blocos throughout the city.
- **Salvador (Bahia)**: centered on the **trio elétrico**, a truck-mounted stage that crawls through the crowd blasting live **axé** music, with fans following on foot inside a roped-off area (paid, via **abadá**) or outside it in the free-roaming **pipoca**.
- **Recife and Olinda (Pernambuco)**: known for **frevo** — fast, acrobatic dancing with small umbrellas — plus giant papier-mâché puppet parades (bonecos gigantes) through Olinda's colonial streets, with a much less commercialized, more neighborhood feel than Rio or Salvador.

A learner planning a trip should know these aren't interchangeable: going to "Carnival in Brazil" without picking a city can mean three completely different trips.

title: Practical safety and crowd norms
bodyMd:
Carnival crowds are dense, loud, and long — some blocos and trios elétricos move for hours through packed streets in full sun. A few practical norms visitors quickly pick up:

- Phone signal frequently drops in the biggest crowds. Agree on a physical meeting point with friends beforehand (a landmark, not just "I'll text you") — see the example dialogue about meeting near the *corda*.
- Carry as little as possible: a small crossbody bag or a pouch worn in front, not a backpack, is the norm — pickpocketing in dense crowds is a real risk.
- **Se hidratar** (hydrating) and wearing **protetor solar** aren't afterthoughts — many blocos run for 6+ hours outdoors in tropical heat.
- Being groped or crowded uninvited is unfortunately common enough that most cities now run visible awareness campaigns against it ("não é não" — "no means no"); it is not something to shrug off as "part of Carnival," and calling it out or moving away is completely normal.
- If you buy an **abadá**, it's your ticket inside a cordoned trio elétrico's **corda** — losing it usually means you can't get back in.

title: What the escola de samba competition is actually about
bodyMd:
An escola de samba isn't just a parade group — it's a year-round community institution, usually tied to a specific neighborhood or favela, that treats its yearly parade as a competitive artistic statement. Every element is judged: the **samba-enredo** (the song), the **enredo** (the theme/storyline, often historical, mythological, or pointedly political), the **carro alegórico** floats, each **ala**'s costumes, the **bateria**'s precision, and the **mestre-sala**/**porta-bandeira** pair's presentation of the school's flag.

The **enredo** matters more than it might first appear: escolas have used their one parade slot to tell stories about slavery and resistance, Indigenous history, or current political figures, turning a night of music and costumes into pointed public commentary watched by millions. Losing points, or the competition outright, is taken seriously — relegation to a lower division is a real consequence for a school that scores poorly.

title: Abadá, camarote, or pipoca — picking how you experience it
bodyMd:
Especially in Salvador's street Carnival, there isn't just one way to be part of the crowd — there are tiers:

- **Abadá**: buying the branded shirt for a specific bloco or trio elétrico gets you inside its roped-off **corda**, with more space, security, and often bathrooms/bars nearby.
- **Camarote**: an elevated, ticketed VIP structure overlooking the route — the most comfortable, least chaotic way to watch, at the highest price.
- **Pipoca**: no ticket, no abadá — just being part of the free-roaming crowd outside the cordon. It's the cheapest and, for many Brazilians, the most fun way to do it, though it's denser and less controlled.

None of these is the "correct" way to experience Carnival — Brazilians move between all three depending on the year, the budget, and who they're with.

title: How Carnival actually ends
bodyMd:
Carnival has a hard stop: **quarta-feira de cinzas** (Ash Wednesday), the start of Lent on the Catholic calendar. Officially the partying runs Friday through Tuesday (Fat Tuesday), though in practice big cities are already in full swing the weekend before.

The day after is treated almost like a national institution of its own — **ressaca de carnaval** isn't just about the drinking, it covers the collective exhaustion of days spent dancing outdoors in the heat. Shops and offices often ease back in gently, and "como foi seu carnaval?" ("how was your Carnival?") is a standard piece of small talk for the following week or two, the same way "how was your vacation?" would be elsewhere.
